enrichment.short_description: A distributed NoSQL database built for high-throughput, low-latency, data-intensive applications.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ScyllaDB is an open-source, DynamoDB-compatible NoSQL database for high-throughput, low-latency workloads at massive scale.

features.title: Shard-per-core architecture
features.description: Designed for high throughput with predictable single-digit millisecond latencies at large scale.

features.title: DynamoDB-compatible API
features.description: ScyllaDB Alternator exposes a DynamoDB-compatible API so DynamoDB workloads can run against ScyllaDB.

features.title: Vector search
features.description: Native vector search capability aimed at retrieval-augmented generation, agentic, and semantic search use cases.

features.title: Kubernetes operator
features.description: The ScyllaDB Operator supports deploying and managing ScyllaDB clusters on Kubernetes.

features.title: Feature store support
features.description: Optimized for machine learning feature stores that require scale and low latency.

pros: Open-source core available for self-hosting
DynamoDB-compatible API eases migration from DynamoDB
Built for high throughput and predictable low latency at billion-row scale
Offers both a fully managed cloud service and a self-managed Enterprise edition
cons: ScyllaDB Cloud and Enterprise pricing is quote-only and not published
Distributed NoSQL operations require specialized expertise to run well
Advanced HA/DR and security features are reserved for higher-tier paid plans

faqs.question: Is ScyllaDB open source?
faqs.answer: ScyllaDB has an open-source component available on GitHub, alongside a self-managed Enterprise edition and a fully managed ScyllaDB Cloud service.

faqs.question: Can ScyllaDB replace DynamoDB?
faqs.answer: ScyllaDB Alternator provides a DynamoDB-compatible API, allowing applications built for DynamoDB to run against ScyllaDB.

faqs.question: Does ScyllaDB support Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, the ScyllaDB Operator is available for deploying and managing ScyllaDB clusters on Kubernetes.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for ScyllaDB Cloud?
faqs.answer: ScyllaDB offers a 30-day developer trial and a 48-hour production evaluation for ScyllaDB Cloud.

use_cases.title: Real-time AI and ML inference
use_cases.description: Serving feature stores and low-latency lookups for machine learning inference pipelines.

use_cases.title: High-throughput event-driven apps
use_cases.description: Backing applications that need millions of operations per second with predictable latency.

use_cases.title: DynamoDB migration
use_cases.description: Moving DynamoDB-based workloads to a self-hosted or multi-cloud database via the Alternator API.
use_cases.audience: Teams reducing DynamoDB lock-in or cost

features: Self-managed deployment on-prem, VM, or Kubernetes

long_form_blocks.heading: What is ScyllaDB?
paragraphs: ScyllaDB is a distributed NoSQL database built for data-intensive applications that need high throughput and predictable low latency, with an open-source core plus self-managed Enterprise and fully managed Cloud editions.
It offers a DynamoDB-compatible API (Alternator), a Kubernetes operator, and native vector search aimed at retrieval-augmented generation and other AI workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ScyllaDB is for
paragraphs: ScyllaDB fits teams running large-scale, latency-sensitive workloads such as real-time personalization, event-driven systems, and ML feature stores, and organizations looking to migrate off DynamoDB while keeping the same API shape.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, column-oriented SQL database built for fast analytical (OLAP) queries at scale.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClickHouse is an open-source, column-oriented database for real-time analytics, offering both self-managed and ClickHouse Cloud deployment.

features.title: Column-oriented storage
features.description: Stores data by column rather than row, accelerating analytical queries over large datasets.

features.title: High compression
features.description: Column-oriented storage enables strong compression ratios that reduce storage footprint and speed up queries.

features.title: Standard SQL
features.description: Queries are written in SQL, making it accessible to teams already familiar with relational databases.

features.title: 100+ integrations
features.description: Connects to BI tools, data visualization platforms, and language clients.

features.title: ClickHouse Local
features.description: A local mode for querying files directly without running a server.

pros: Free, fully open-source self-managed option
Delivers fast query performance on large datasets via columnar storage
Wide ecosystem of integrations with BI and observability tools
Managed ClickHouse Cloud option removes operational overhead
Used in production by large organizations including Meta, Spotify, and Lyft
cons: ClickHouse Cloud pricing is not published on the pricing page and requires contacting sales for specifics
Column-oriented design is optimized for analytics, not transactional (OLTP) workloads
Self-managed deployments require infrastructure and operations expertise

faqs.question: Is ClickHouse free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-managed, open-source version of ClickHouse is free to download and run; costs depend only on the infrastructure you run it on.

faqs.question: Does ClickHouse offer a managed cloud service?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClickHouse Cloud is a paid managed service available on AWS, GCP, and Azure.

faqs.question: What is ClickHouse best suited for?
faqs.answer: ClickHouse is designed for real-time analytics, dashboards, observability data, and data warehousing rather than transactional workloads.

faqs.question: Can I query files without a ClickHouse server?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClickHouse Local lets you query files directly using ClickHouse's SQL engine without deploying a server.

use_cases.title: Real-time analytics dashboards
use_cases.description: Powering dashboards that need fast aggregate queries over large, continuously updated datasets.

use_cases.title: Observability data storage
use_cases.description: Storing and querying high-volume logs, metrics, and traces.

use_cases.title: Data warehousing
use_cases.description: Serving as a SQL-based analytical warehouse for reporting and BI tools.

features: Fully managed on AWS, GCP, or Azure

long_form_blocks.heading: What is ClickHouse?
paragraphs: ClickHouse is a fast, open-source, column-oriented SQL database management system designed for online analytical processing (OLAP), enabling real-time queries over petabyte-scale datasets.
It can be self-managed for free or run as ClickHouse Cloud, a fully managed service on AWS, GCP, and Azure with automatic compute autoscaling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ClickHouse is for
paragraphs: ClickHouse suits data and platform teams building real-time analytics, observability pipelines, or SQL-based data warehouses that need to query very large datasets quickly.

enrichment.short_description: An in-process, open-source SQL OLAP database engine for fast analytics on local files and cloud storage.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DuckDB is a free, MIT-licensed, in-process SQL OLAP database for querying Parquet, CSV, JSON, and cloud storage directly.

features.title: In-process execution
features.description: Runs embedded inside an application with no separate server process required.

features.title: Multi-format querying
features.description: Queries Parquet, CSV, and JSON files, as well as cloud storage such as S3, Azure, and GCP, directly with SQL.

features.title: Columnar storage engine
features.description: Uses columnar storage with disk-spilling to handle datasets larger than available memory.

features.title: Extension architecture
features.description: Supports extensions that add custom functionality beyond the core engine.

features.title: WebAssembly support
features.description: Can run inside web browsers via WebAssembly for client-side analytics.

pros: MIT-licensed and completely free with no licensing costs
No server setup required — runs embedded in-process
Native client libraries for Python, Go, Rust, JavaScript, Java, C, C++, and R
Can query remote and cloud-stored files without moving data first
Runs on Linux, macOS, Windows, and in the browser via WebAssembly
cons: In-process design means it is not built as a multi-user networked database server by default
Best suited to analytical (OLAP) queries rather than high-concurrency transactional workloads
Very large distributed workloads may require a different architecture than a single-process engine

faqs.question: Is DuckDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, DuckDB is released under the MIT license and is free to use with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: Do I need a server to run DuckDB?
faqs.answer: No, DuckDB runs in-process, embedded directly inside the application or script that uses it.

faqs.question: What file formats can DuckDB query?
faqs.answer: DuckDB can query Parquet, CSV, and JSON files, as well as data stored in S3, Azure, and GCP, directly using SQL.

faqs.question: Which languages does DuckDB support?
faqs.answer: DuckDB provides native client libraries for Python, Go, Rust, JavaScript, Java, C, C++, R, and a command-line interface.

use_cases.title: Local analytics on data files
use_cases.description: Running SQL analytics directly against Parquet, CSV, or JSON files without loading them into a separate database.

use_cases.title: Embedded analytics in applications
use_cases.description: Adding an analytical query engine directly inside a Python, Java, or JavaScript application.

use_cases.title: Browser-based analytics
use_cases.description: Running SQL queries client-side in the browser via the WebAssembly build.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is DuckDB?
paragraphs: DuckDB is an in-process SQL OLAP database management system released under the MIT license, designed to run analytical queries directly against data files and cloud storage without a separate server.
It offers native client libraries for languages including Python, Go, Rust, JavaScript, Java, C, C++, and R, and can run on Linux, macOS, Windows, or in the browser via WebAssembly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who DuckDB is for
paragraphs: DuckDB fits data analysts and developers who want fast SQL analytics on local files or cloud storage without standing up a database server, and application developers embedding analytical queries directly in their software.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source proxy that translates the MongoDB wire protocol to PostgreSQL, offering a MongoDB-compatible database.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FerretDB is an open-source, MongoDB-compatible database that converts MongoDB wire protocol queries to PostgreSQL, avoiding SSPL licensing.

features.title: MongoDB wire protocol compatibility
features.description: Converts MongoDB 5.0+ wire protocol queries into SQL, letting existing MongoDB drivers connect to FerretDB.

features.title: PostgreSQL-backed engine
features.description: Uses PostgreSQL with the DocumentDB extension as its underlying storage engine.

features.title: Open-source licensing
features.description: Built as a license-unrestricted alternative after MongoDB moved to the Server Side Public License (SSPL).

features.title: Managed cloud option
features.description: FerretDB Cloud provides a hosted version of FerretDB via a separate signup portal.

pros: Open-source with no SSPL-style licensing restrictions
Lets teams reuse existing MongoDB drivers and tooling
Built on PostgreSQL, a mature and widely supported database engine
Both self-hosted and managed cloud deployment options are available
cons: MongoDB compatibility is still being expanded, so some commands or behaviors may differ from MongoDB
FerretDB Cloud pricing is not published
Teams must verify feature-by-feature compatibility before relying on it for production MongoDB workloads

faqs.question: Is FerretDB a drop-in replacement for MongoDB?
faqs.answer: FerretDB aims to support MongoDB 5.0+ wire protocol commands, but documentation notes known differences, so compatibility should be verified for each use case before production deployment.

faqs.question: What database does FerretDB run on?
faqs.answer: FerretDB uses PostgreSQL with the DocumentDB extension as its underlying storage engine.

faqs.question: Why was FerretDB created?
faqs.answer: FerretDB was created as an open-source alternative to MongoDB after MongoDB moved to the Server Side Public License (SSPL).

faqs.question: Is there a managed version of FerretDB?
faqs.answer: Yes, FerretDB Cloud offers a hosted version of FerretDB alongside the self-hosted open-source option.

use_cases.title: MongoDB license migration
use_cases.description: Replacing MongoDB deployments that need an open-source, non-SSPL document database.

use_cases.title: PostgreSQL-based document workloads
use_cases.description: Running document-style queries on top of a PostgreSQL infrastructure teams already operate.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is FerretDB?
paragraphs: FerretDB is an open-source proxy that converts MongoDB 5.0+ wire protocol queries into SQL, running on PostgreSQL with the DocumentDB extension as its storage engine.
It was created as a license-unrestricted alternative to MongoDB after MongoDB adopted the Server Side Public License (SSPL), and is available both self-hosted and as a managed FerretDB Cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who FerretDB is for
paragraphs: FerretDB is aimed at developers and organizations that want MongoDB-style document database compatibility without SSPL licensing terms, particularly teams already running PostgreSQL infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: A BSD-licensed, open-source key-value data store supported by the Linux Foundation as a permanent open-source fork.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Valkey is a free, open-source, BSD-licensed key-value data store backed by the Linux Foundation for caching and real-time workloads.

features.title: Rich data structures
features.description: Supports strings, numbers, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, and hyperloglogs.

features.title: Lua scripting and modules
features.description: Supports Lua scripting and module-based extensibility.

features.title: Cluster and replication support
features.description: Can run as a standalone daemon or in cluster mode with replication and high-availability options.

features.title: Linux Foundation governance
features.description: Governed by LF Projects, LLC under the Linux Foundation, which manages trademarks and open-source policy.

features.title: Multi-cloud managed availability
features.description: Available as a managed service from multiple cloud providers, including AWS, Google Cloud, and Oracle.

pros: Fully open source under a permissive BSD license
Backed by the Linux Foundation, giving it vendor-neutral governance
Supports caching, message queuing, and primary database use cases
Available as a managed service from several major cloud providers
cons: No official paid tier or support contract directly from the Valkey project itself
As a newer fork, feature parity with Redis extensions may vary over time
Cluster and HA configuration require operational expertise to run reliably

faqs.question: Is Valkey free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Valkey is open source under a BSD license with no licensing costs.

faqs.question: Who governs the Valkey project?
faqs.answer: Valkey is backed by the Linux Foundation through LF Projects, LLC, which manages the project's trademarks and policies.

faqs.question: Can Valkey run in a cluster?
faqs.answer: Yes, Valkey supports standalone and cluster deployment modes with replication and high-availability options.

faqs.question: Is Valkey available as a managed service?
faqs.answer: Yes, several cloud providers, including AWS, Google Cloud, and Oracle, offer managed Valkey services.

use_cases.title: Application caching
use_cases.description: Using Valkey as an in-memory cache to reduce load on primary databases.

use_cases.title: Real-time and low-latency workloads
use_cases.description: Serving low-latency reads and writes for real-time application features.

use_cases.title: Message queuing
use_cases.description: Using Valkey's data structures to implement lightweight message queues.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Valkey?
paragraphs: Valkey is an open-source, BSD-licensed key-value data store backed by the Linux Foundation, positioned as a permanently open-source project that supports caching, message queuing, and primary database use cases.
It supports data structures such as strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, and hyperloglogs, along with Lua scripting, modules, and cluster/replication deployment modes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Valkey is for
paragraphs: Valkey suits teams that want a vendor-neutral, permissively licensed in-memory data store for caching or low-latency workloads, including those seeking an open-source alternative in the Redis ecosystem.

enrichment.short_description: A Redis-compatible, multi-threaded in-memory data store built for higher throughput on modern cloud hardware.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dragonfly is a Redis-compatible, multi-threaded in-memory data store, available open source or as managed Dragonfly Cloud from $36/month.

features.title: Thread-per-core architecture
features.description: Uses a multi-threaded, shared-nothing design that utilizes every core, unlike Redis's single-threaded model.

features.title: Redis API compatibility
features.description: Compatible with the Redis protocol, allowing Redis clients to connect to Dragonfly.

features.title: Higher throughput per instance
features.description: The vendor reports significantly higher queries-per-second than Redis on comparable hardware, reducing the number of instances needed.

features.title: Managed cloud service
features.description: Dragonfly Cloud offers fully managed deployment on AWS, GCP, and Azure with one-click provisioning.

pros: Open source and free to self-host
Redis protocol compatibility eases migration for existing Redis users
Multi-threaded architecture can reduce the number of instances needed versus Redis clusters
Managed Dragonfly Cloud plans start at a published price ($36/month Flex plan)
Free trial credit offered for Dragonfly Cloud
cons: Business and Enterprise cloud tiers are priced much higher ($2,000/month and quote-based respectively)
Performance claims are vendor-published benchmarks and may vary by workload
As a newer project, its operational track record is shorter than Redis's

faqs.question: Is Dragonfly compatible with Redis?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dragonfly is compatible with the Redis protocol, so existing Redis clients can connect to it.

faqs.question: How much does Dragonfly Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Dragonfly Cloud's Flex plan starts at $36/month; Business starts at $2,000/month; Enterprise pricing is quote-based.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Dragonfly Cloud?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dragonfly offers a free trial with credit for the Flex plan.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Dragonfly for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source version of Dragonfly can be self-deployed at no cost.

use_cases.title: High-performance caching
use_cases.description: Replacing Redis clusters with fewer Dragonfly instances for caching workloads.

use_cases.title: Real-time feature stores
use_cases.description: Serving low-latency feature lookups for real-time applications.

use_cases.title: Real-time leaderboards and message queues
use_cases.description: Powering leaderboard and queue-based application features that need high throughput.

features: Fully managed on AWS, GCP, or Azure

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Dragonfly?
paragraphs: Dragonfly is a Redis-protocol-compatible, in-memory data store built with a thread-per-core, shared-nothing architecture designed to use every core on modern cloud hardware.
It's available as free open-source software for self-hosting, or as Dragonfly Cloud, a managed service with published plans starting at $36/month for the Flex tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dragonfly is for
paragraphs: Dragonfly fits teams currently running Redis who want to consolidate multiple Redis instances or clusters into fewer, higher-throughput Dragonfly instances, whether self-hosted or via the managed cloud service.

enrichment.short_description: A fully open-source, multithreaded, drop-in alternative to Redis maintained by Snap Inc.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KeyDB is a free, open-source, multithreaded, Redis-compatible in-memory data store maintained by Snap Inc.

features.title: Multithreaded engine
features.description: A multithreaded architecture enabling over 1 million operations per second per node.

features.title: Redis compatibility
features.description: Functions as a drop-in alternative to Redis with support for the same data structures, including strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and streams.

features.title: MVCC concurrency
features.description: Uses a multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) architecture that allows concurrent queries without blocking.

features.title: Active-active replication
features.description: Supports cross-region multi-master replication.

features.title: ModJS scripting
features.description: Allows custom commands to be written in JavaScript using the V8 engine.

pros: Fully open source under a BSD-3 license with no cost to self-host
Multithreaded design targets higher throughput than single-threaded Redis
Backed and used in production by Snap Inc.'s caching infrastructure
Supports cross-region multi-master (active-active) replication
cons: Latest GitHub release (6.3.4) dates to October 2023, indicating a slower release cadence recently
No managed cloud offering from the KeyDB project itself
Open-source and Enterprise editions were consolidated into a single project, which may affect availability of prior Enterprise-only features

faqs.question: Is KeyDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeyDB is a fully open-source project with no licensing cost, released under a BSD-3 license.

faqs.question: Who maintains KeyDB?
faqs.answer: KeyDB is maintained by Snap Inc., which also uses it as part of its own caching infrastructure.

faqs.question: How is KeyDB different from Redis?
faqs.answer: KeyDB uses a multithreaded architecture, unlike Redis's traditionally single-threaded design, aiming for higher throughput while maintaining Redis compatibility.

faqs.question: Does KeyDB support active-active replication?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeyDB supports cross-region multi-master replication.

use_cases.title: High-throughput caching
use_cases.description: Replacing Redis in caching layers that need higher per-node throughput.

use_cases.title: Multi-region active-active deployments
use_cases.description: Running replicated data stores across regions with multi-master writes.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is KeyDB?
paragraphs: KeyDB is a fully open-source, multithreaded, in-memory data store built as a drop-in alternative to Redis, maintained by Snap Inc. and used as part of its caching infrastructure.
It combines Redis-compatible data structures and commands with a multithreaded engine, MVCC concurrency, and support for cross-region multi-master replication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who KeyDB is for
paragraphs: KeyDB is suited to teams already using Redis who want a compatible, free, multithreaded alternative capable of higher per-node throughput and multi-region active-active replication.

enrichment.short_description: An Apache 2.0-licensed, open-source search, analytics, and observability suite governed by the Linux Foundation.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenSearch is a free, Apache 2.0-licensed, open-source search and analytics suite for search, observability, and security use cases.

features.title: OpenSearch Core
features.description: A search and analytics engine built on Apache Lucene.

features.title: OpenSearch Dashboards
features.description: Data visualization and exploration tools for OpenSearch data.

features.title: OpenSearch Data Prepper
features.description: A server-side data collector for enriching and transforming data before indexing.

features.title: Vector engine for AI/ML
features.description: A vector engine supporting vector search and anomaly detection for AI and ML applications.

features.title: Security analytics
features.description: Supports threat intelligence and event correlation use cases.

pros: Fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license
Governed by the Linux Foundation via the OpenSearch Software Foundation
Covers search, observability, and security analytics in one project
Includes a vector engine for AI/ML search use cases
Active community across forums, Slack, and GitHub
cons: No pricing or managed-service details are provided directly by the OpenSearch project itself
Running OpenSearch at scale requires operational expertise or a third-party managed service
Multiple components (Core, Dashboards, Data Prepper, Vector Engine) add deployment complexity

faqs.question: Is OpenSearch free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenSearch is open source under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: Who governs the OpenSearch project?
faqs.answer: OpenSearch is governed by the OpenSearch Software Foundation under the Linux Foundation, established in September 2024.

faqs.question: Does OpenSearch support vector search?
faqs.answer: Yes, the OpenSearch Vector Engine supports vector search and anomaly detection for AI and machine learning applications.

faqs.question: What is OpenSearch Dashboards?
faqs.answer: OpenSearch Dashboards is the data visualization and exploration component of the OpenSearch suite.

use_cases.title: Enterprise and e-commerce search
use_cases.description: Powering document and product search experiences.

use_cases.title: Observability
use_cases.description: Log analysis and performance monitoring across systems.

use_cases.title: Security analytics
use_cases.description: Threat intelligence and security event correlation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenSearch?
paragraphs: OpenSearch is an open-source, Apache 2.0-licensed search and analytics suite governed by the OpenSearch Software Foundation under the Linux Foundation, combining a Lucene-based search engine (OpenSearch Core) with Dashboards, Data Prepper, and a Vector Engine.
It targets search, observability, security analytics, and AI/ML use cases such as vector search and anomaly detection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenSearch is for
paragraphs: OpenSearch fits teams that need an open-source, vendor-neutral platform for search, log and metrics observability, or security analytics, without relying on a single-vendor search product.

name: ZincSearch
skip_reason: The official site (zincsearch.com) now permanently redirects through zinc.dev to openobserve.ai, a differently branded product. The ZincSearch GitHub repo's own README steers log-search users to the separate OpenObserve project, and its latest release (v0.4.10) dates to January 2024, well over two years stale — indicating the original product has effectively been superseded/rebranded rather than actively promoted under its own name.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source, Rust-based search and analytics engine built on Tantivy, optimized for cloud object storage.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Quickwit is an open-source, Rust-based search engine built on Tantivy for sub-second search directly on cloud object storage.

features.title: Decoupled compute and storage
features.description: Separates compute from storage so search runs directly against cloud object storage such as S3, MinIO, or Ceph.

features.title: Optimized file format
features.description: Uses a custom file format that reduces I/O requests against object storage.

features.title: Native observability protocol support
features.description: Supports OpenTelemetry and Jaeger natively for log and trace ingestion.

features.title: Multi-tenancy with retention policies
features.description: Supports multi-tenant indexes with retention and lifecycle policies, plus GDPR-compliant deletion.

features.title: Horizontally scalable, high-availability design
features.description: Built to run in single- or multi-node configurations that scale horizontally.

pros: Free, open-source, community-based software
Built in Rust on the Tantivy search library for performance
Searches directly against cheap object storage instead of requiring costly local disks
Native OpenTelemetry and Jaeger support suits observability and log-management use cases
Continued as open source with plans to move to the Apache 2.0 license after Quickwit's team joined Datadog
cons: Quickwit's founding team joined Datadog, shifting primary company focus toward a new Datadog product rather than a standalone Quickwit commercial offering
No published managed-cloud pricing tiers for Quickwit itself
As the project transitions ownership/licensing, roadmap and support commitments may evolve

faqs.question: Is Quickwit still maintained after joining Datadog?
faqs.answer: Yes, according to Quickwit's own announcement, the open-source engine continues to be developed, with a new version planned under the Apache 2.0 license, even as the founding team also works on a new product with Datadog.

faqs.question: Is Quickwit free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Quickwit is free, open-source, community-based software available on GitHub.

faqs.question: What is Quickwit built on?
faqs.answer: Quickwit is built in Rust and powered by the Tantivy search library.

faqs.question: Can Quickwit search data directly on object storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, Quickwit's decoupled compute-and-storage architecture is designed to run sub-second search directly against cloud object storage like S3, MinIO, and Ceph.

use_cases.title: Log management
use_cases.description: Ingesting and searching high-volume logs stored on cost-efficient object storage.

use_cases.title: Distributed tracing
use_cases.description: Storing and querying trace data ingested via OpenTelemetry or Jaeger.

use_cases.title: Full-text search on cloud storage
use_cases.description: Running full-text search applications without maintaining local search-node disks.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Quickwit?
paragraphs: Quickwit is an open-source search and analytics engine, written in Rust and powered by the Tantivy library, designed to deliver sub-second search directly against cloud object storage such as S3, MinIO, and Ceph.
Quickwit's founding team joined Datadog; the company has said the open-source engine will continue under the Apache 2.0 license even as it also builds a new product with Datadog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Quickwit is for
paragraphs: Quickwit suits teams that want log management, distributed tracing, or full-text search running directly against low-cost object storage rather than maintaining expensive local search-node disks.
